title: onRangeNegotiated callback silently not fired on abort-after-headers early return
description: When the AbortSignal is already aborted at the second pre-body check (line 521, after headers and reader are acquired), the function does `return makeInfo(0)` directly, bypassing the inner try/finally block at line 662. The `onRangeNegotiated?.(finalInfo)` call at line 685 is only inside that finally block, so it is never invoked for this exit path. The documented contract for `onRangeNegotiated` is 'fired once from finally after the transfer completes or fails mid-body'; an abort that arrives after response headers but before the read loop starts is a mid-body failure, not a pre-fetch failure like 416/503, so callers with an `onRangeNegotiated` listener receive no notification and cannot observe the partial negotiation result.

title: Abort event listener not removed in four validation-throw paths between addEventListener and the body-loop try/finally
description: The abort listener is registered at line 525. The four validation-failure throws at lines 532-538 (resume 200-fallback), 543-547 (ETag mismatch on 206), 550-554 (range-start mismatch), and 563-567 (malformed Content-Range end) each call `cancelBoth()` and throw, but none calls `signal?.removeEventListener('abort', onAbort)` before throwing. The inner try/finally at line 662 (which does the removeEventListener) is never entered. As a result the abort listener remains registered for the lifetime of the signal, holding closure references to `session`, `reader`, and other locals. A long-lived `AbortController` used with many failing resume requests accumulates leaked listeners. The listener is idempotent when fired (cancelBoth is guarded by the `cancelled` flag), so there is no incorrect behaviour—only a resource/GC leak.

title: resumeFromByteRange returns honored:true for the no-op completed-state path where no HTTP request was made
description: When `state.start === state.endExclusive` (transfer already complete), the function short-circuits with `return { requested: 0, honored: true, delivered: 0, ... }`. No fetch was issued, so `honored` is meaningless, but hardcoding `true` tells callers that a Range response was honored—which is false. A caller checking `info.honored` to decide, say, whether to trust `fullSize` from Content-Range would be misled. The value should be `false` (or omitted / undefined) to reflect that no server interaction occurred, matching the semantics of `honored: false` used for the 200-fallback path.

title: No test for abort-after-headers (signal already aborted at the second pre-body guard) to pin the onRangeNegotiated contract
description: The test suite covers pre-fetch abort (signal aborted before fetch is called) and mid-read abort (signal fired during body streaming), but there is no test for the case where the signal is already aborted at the second guard inside `fromByteRange` (line 521)—i.e., after `resp.body.getReader()` succeeds but before the body read loop starts. This path contains the `onRangeNegotiated`-not-fired bug (logic-1), but without a test the contract is invisible and a fix could be accidentally reverted. A property-based or parametric test covering the 'abort arrives between headers and body-loop start' timing would catch regressions in teardown order.

title: BufferedReader.take() is not tested with negative size argument
description: The `take(size: number)` implementation guards `if (size < 0) return null` at line 128, but none of the existing `BufferedReader` tests exercise a negative argument. This guard is the only path that returns `null` without any internal state mutation. Without a test for it, a future refactor (e.g., collapsing the `size < 0` and `total < size` checks into a single condition) could accidentally break the negative-guard contract—especially since `takeAll()` calls `this.take(this.total)` and relies on non-null return.
